segments.s144-g105: سواران زابل is a nominative noun phrase meaning “the riders/horsemen of Zabul” (the place name follows the noun to indicate affiliation). شنیدند is 3rd-person plural simple past (“they heard”); in this poetic line the direct object نای (the ney/flute) appears after the verb rather than before, which is a common flexible/poetic word order in classical Persian.
segments.s144-g106: برفتند is 3rd-person plural past of برفتن (to go off, depart, or rise up) and governs the following adverbial complements. با introduces accompaniment or manner (“with”), linking ترگ و جوشن (two coordinated nouns: ترگ, an archaic/poetic ‘rag’ or torn cloth, and جوشن, ‘armor/ cuirass’). ز جای uses the poetic short form ز (for از) + جای “place,” meaning “from their places/positions” and together these phrases specify how and from where they departed.
segments.s144-g33: Word order: indirect object + addressee (به گیو) + temporal adverb (آنگهی) + reported-speech verb (گفت) followed by the quoted imperative. آنگهی is a literary/archaic adverb meaning 'then' or 'thereupon'. گفت is the past tense 'said' introducing the command برسان دود. برسان is an imperative/infinitive stem meaning 'bring/produce', and دود is the object 'smoke' (here 'smoke signal').
segments.s144-g34: This is a short predicate clause lacking an explicit copula: عنان تگاور is a genitive noun phrase 'the reins of the rider/horseman' (classical poetry often omits the ezafe). بباید is a modal 'must/ought to', and بسود is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to advantage' or 'for benefit'. The whole clause states obligation about the reins (i.e. they should be handled to secure advantage).
segments.s144-g35: بباید is the modal 'must/ought' governing the subordinate clause introduced by که. نزدیک رستم شوی contains the adjective/adv. نزدیک 'near' followed by the proper name رستم and the verb شوی (2nd‑person singular subjunctive/imperative form of شدن/شدن), here functioning as 'you should become/come near'. The structure = 'You must draw near to Rostam.'
segments.s144-g36: به زابل نمانی is an admonition: به + place (زابل) + نمانی (2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive of ماندن) = 'do not remain in Zabul' (in poetry به can mark location/direction). و‌گر introduces an alternative/conditional 'or if' and links to نغنوی (غنودن 'to sleep'): 'and if you sleep' / 'nor fall asleep'. The modal بباید from the previous clause scopes here as well (imperative/obligation).
segments.s144-g37: Conditional clause: اگر + temporal noun شب + verb رسی (2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive/optative of رسیدن) = 'if night arrives/comes'. روز را بازگرد is an imperative: روز (day) marked by را (emphatic/definite object/temporal phrase) + بازگرد (imperative 'return'/'go back'), here meaning 'return in/by day'. The verb for the return is imperative and placed after the time phrase as typical in Persian.
segments.s144-g38: بگویش is the imperative 'tell him' (بگو + enclitic ش = 'to him'). که introduces the content clause. تنگ اندرآمد نبرد is a concise, idiomatic poetic phrase: تنگ modifies the following verbal notion (اندرآمد here used verbally in an older/poetic sense 'came/entered' or 'grew'), and نبرد (battle) is the noun. Together: 'tell him that the battle has become tight/critical.' Note اندرآمد here is classical/poetic and does not match modern noun senses; its verbal sense ('came into' / 'became') is understood from context.
segments.s144-g39: وگرنه 'otherwise' introduces the alternative conclusion. فرازست is a predicate meaning 'is high/exalted' (fused predicative adjective + copula). این مرد گرد is the topical noun phrase 'this man' with گرد as a poetic epithet attached to مرد; in classical word order the predicate follows for emphasis. The clause states that, in the other case, 'this man' stands high/has status.
segments.s144-g40: بداندیش را marks the object (the ill‑minded man) with را. خوار نتوان شمرد is a negated verbal phrase: نتوان (cannot) + شمرد (literally 'to count' but here in the sense 'to regard/consider'); خوار (lowly/despised) is the predicate complement. Word order places the modal/negative before the main verb, typical in classical Persian: 'one cannot consider (him) despicable.'
segments.s144-g25: بدان‌گونه is an adverbial phrase ('in that manner') placed at the front for emphasis. دیدند is a 3rd-person plural past tense verb ('they saw' — a standard epic narrative past). گردان نیو is a noun phrase: گردان ('troop') is the head noun followed by the name/epithet نیو without an explicit ezāfe (poetic/older syntax often omits the ezāfe), so the phrase means 'Niv's troop' or 'the troop called Niv'.
segments.s144-g26: که introduces a subordinate (content) clause reporting what was seen. پیش تو is a prepositional phrase meaning 'before you / in your presence' (the preposition precedes the pronoun). آید is 3rd-person singular present (used in narrative to mean 'comes' or 'will come'); it is the verb of the subordinate clause. گرانمایه is an epithet placed before the proper name (poetic word order) and modifies گیو, the subject/name at the clause end.
segments.s144-g27: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. نامه is the direct object of the verb that follows. بخوانی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive used in temporal clauses after چو: 'when you read/recite'. به‌روز و به شب are adverbial time phrases ('by day and by night'); به here makes the nouns adverbial ('by/at').
segments.s144-g28: مکن is a prohibitive imperative 'do not'. داستان را is the definite direct object marked by را. گشاده دو لب is a manner phrase: گشاده ('open') modifies the noun phrase دو لب ('two lips' = 'mouth / lips'), literally 'with lips open' — idiomatically 'openly / aloud'. The whole phrase gives the manner of speaking the tale (i.e. do not speak it openly).
segments.s144-g29: مگر here functions as 'unless / except that' introducing the condition. با introduces the instrument/companions 'with'. سواران is the plural noun 'horsemen/riders', and بسیارهوش is a compound adjective 'very clever / highly intelligent' describing those horsemen.
segments.s144-g30: ز زابل is a prepositional phrase 'from Zabul' (ز is the poetic form of از). برانی here is used as a verbal form meaning 'drive away / expel' (an older poetic present/subjunctive/optative form derived from راندن); although the supplied senses list lacks an explicit 'drive' sense, it functions verbally. برآری is a verb meaning 'you bring about/produce' or 'you raise' and governs خروش 'a cry/uproar' as its object: 'you will bring forth a cry.' Word order places the place phrase before the verbs (poetic order).
segments.s144-g31: بر اینسان is a fixed idiom 'by/on this account; in this way'. که introduces the subordinate content clause. گژدهم is a proper name (Gozdahm/Gozdaham). ز + و represent the older contracted form 'زو' (= از + او) meaning 'of him / from him' (poetic contraction). یاد کرد is a compound meaning 'remembered' (یاد + کرد, where کرد is the auxiliary/past marker making the action past).
segments.s144-g32: نباید is impersonal 'there ought not to be / it should not be' (negative necessity). جز ... از تو sets up the exception 'except you' (جز followed by a prepositional phrase). ورا is a dative/locative particle meaning 'to him / for him' (poetic; corresponds to 'به او'), and هم‌نبرد is a substantive 'comrade‑in‑arms / companion in battle' — the whole clause says there should be no comrade for him except you.
segments.s144-g49: ز اسپ is a prepositional phrase (ز = 'from') followed by the object اسپ ('horse/steed'). اندرآمد is an archaic/poetic past verb 'entered/came down' (here 'dismounted/came down from'), and گو نامدار is a postposed epithet: گو functions as an adjectival element meaning '-speaking' or a descriptive particle attached to نامدار ('renowned'), forming a compound epithet describing the person who descended.
segments.s144-g50: از ایران is the prepositional object 'about/of Iran' (از here marks the topic/thing asked about). بپرسید is a past tense form 'he/they asked' formed with the archaic b- prefix. وز here is the fused poetic sequence و + ز ('and of/from') introducing a second object (شهریار = 'the king'); the printed single token وز functions to link 'king' as the other thing asked about.
segments.s144-g51: ز ره سوی is a locative/directional phrase: ز ('from') + ره ('way, road') + سوی ('toward'), so 'from the road toward ...'. ایوان رستم is the goal ('Rostam's iwan/porch'). شدند is a past plural verb 'they came/they moved' (archaic past form with -ند plural ending) indicating the action of coming/moving.
segments.s144-g52: ببودند is an archaic past/pluperfect-like form 'they were/they remained' (here indicating a brief pause). یکبار is an adverb 'for a moment/once', و is the coordinating 'and', دم ('breath') is used idiomatically for 'moment', and برزدند is a compound past verb (بر- + زدند) 'they struck/attacked' (past, plural). Together the clause means they paused for a breath and then struck.
segments.s144-g53: بگفت is an archaic past 'he said/told' (b- prefix + گفت). آنچ is a relative pronoun 'that which/what', بشنید is past 'he heard', و is 'and', نامه بداد here must be read as 'he gave the letter' (نامه = 'letter'); the token بداد in the edition can look like other nouns but functions here as the verb 'he gave' in the narrative sequence.
segments.s144-g54: ز سهراب is a short prepositional phrase 'about/from Sohrab' (z introducing the topic). چندی is an adverb 'for a while', سخن کرد is the compound verb 'spoke' (سخن = 'speech' + کرد = 'did', i.e. 'uttered words'), and یاد here means 'remembrance/memory' — the clause says he spoke for a while in memory of Sohrab.
segments.s144-g55: تهمتن is the nominative subject (epithet of Rostam). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when', بشنید is past 'he heard', و links the next action, and نامه بخواند is 'he read the letter' (بخواند = past/preterite 'he read' with the archaic b-). The sequence gives two enclosed actions that trigger the following reaction.
segments.s144-g56: بخندید is past 'he laughed', و is 'and', زان ('زان' = ز + آن) means 'because of that / from that', کار 'affair/deed' names the thing causing astonishment, خیره is an adjective 'staring/astonished', and بماند is past 'he remained' (he stayed in that state). Together: he laughed and remained astonished at that event.
segments.s144-g9: گشاینده is a present-active participle ('opener, loosener') used as an epithet; it precedes the noun it modifies. بند here means 'fetter/bond' and is the object of 'گشاینده' (i.e. 'opener of the bonds'). هاماوران is a poetic plural/collective noun 'the level plains' (a geographic epithet) placed after بند to specify which bonds are meant.
segments.s144-g10: ستاننده is another present-active participle ('taker, seizer') functioning in parallel to the previous participle; Persian often strings such epithets without an explicit copula. مرز is 'border/frontier' and مازندران names the region; word order is [verb‑participle] [definite noun phrase], i.e. 'taker of the border of Mazandaran.'
segments.s144-g11: ز is the poetic preposition 'because of / by'; گرز تو ('your mace') is the agentive phrase with the possessor تو after the noun (typical Persian order). خورشید is the subject noun and گریان is a predicative adjective/participle 'weeping' placed before the finite verb شود, which here means 'becomes / is made.' The clause literally: 'From your mace, the sun becomes weeping.'
segments.s144-g12: This clause repeats the same syntactic pattern: ز (because of) + تیغ تو ('your sword') + ناهید (proper noun: the goddess Anahita) + بریان (predicative adjective 'burnt/roasted') + شود ('becomes/is'). The order again places the descriptive adjective before the copular/auxiliary verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s144-g13: چو introduces a simile 'like / as'. گرد here means 'dust' and پی is a postpositive element meaning 'after / in pursuit of' (so گرد پی رخش = 'the dust following his steed'). تو marks the possessor. نیل is a metaphorical reference (literally 'the Nile' or 'blue/peerless thing') placed immediately before the final predicate نیست ('does not exist / there is none'), so the clause reads 'Like the dust after your steed, there is no Nile'—i.e., no comparable thing.
segments.s144-g14: هم‌آورد is a compound noun/adjective meaning 'equal / rival' and it precedes تو ('you') to mark 'one equal to you.' در جهان ('in the world') is a locative phrase; پیل ('elephant') is the noun being negated by the final نیست ('does not exist'). The whole clause: 'An equal to you in the world, an elephant does not exist' → 'There is no elephant in the world that matches you.'
segments.s144-g15: کمند تو ('your lasso') is the noun + possessive construction (possessed noun before تو). بر here is the preposition 'upon/over' attaching to شیر ('lion'). بندافگند is an archaic/compound verbal form meaning 'cast/tied/secured' (the verbal element appears at the end, as Persian typically places the verb at the clause end). The clause reads 'Your lasso upon a lion (it) bound.'
segments.s144-g16: سنان تو ('your spear') is subject + possessor. کوهی here functions as a nominalized adjective 'a mountain (of a mountain)'—i.e. 'a mountain'—followed by the prepositional phrase ز بن ('from the root/base'). The verb برکند is at the end and is a perfective/aorist meaning 'uprooted' or 'tore away.' The clause: 'Your spear uprooted a mountain from its root.'
segments.s144-g1: The clause 'یکی نامه فرمود' is past-tense narrative: 'فرمود' is 3rd-person singular past (used here in the broad sense 'said/ordered/caused'), with its object 'نامه' placed before the verb as is normal in Persian; 'یکی' (a certain/one) is an indefinite determiner modifying 'نامه'. The following 'پس' is a temporal connector 'then' that links to the next clause, and 'شهریار' begins that next clause as its subject ('the king').
segments.s144-g2: 'نوشتن' here is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to write' used in a narrative structure meaning effectively 'wrote'; classical Persian often uses the infinitive form where modern prose uses a finite verb. 'بر' is the preposition 'to/toward' introducing the addressee 'رستم', and 'نامدار' is an adjectival epithet 'renowned' post‑modifying 'رستم'.
segments.s144-g3: 'نخست' is an adverb 'first/at first'. 'آفرین' functions as the noun 'praise/acclamation' and combines with the light verb 'کرد' (past tense 'did/made') to mean 'praised' or 'offered praise'. The preposition 'بر' marks the target/recipient of the praise: 'کردگار' (the Creator/God).
segments.s144-g4: This segment lists epithets linked to the preceding noun: 'جهاندار' is an epithet 'lord/sovereign of the world', 'و' is the coordinating 'and', and 'پرورده روزگار' is a compound participial phrase 'nurtured/produced by (or of) fate/time' where 'پرورده' is the participle 'nurtured' and 'روزگار' denotes 'fate' here.
segments.s144-g5: 'دگر' here means 'again/next'. As in the previous sentence, 'آفرین' + 'کرد' is the light‑verb construction 'praised' (performed the act of acclamation). 'بر' marks the beneficiary/target 'پهلوان' (the hero/champion). Word order is typical: modifier/phrase + light verb + object marked by 'بر'.
segments.s144-g6: The subordinating particle 'که' introduces either a content clause or a hortatory instruction ('that/so that' or 'saying that'). 'بیدار' is an adjective 'awake/alert' modifying 'دل' ('heart'); 'باش' is the imperative 'be' (2nd‑person singular imperative) giving a direct exhortation; 'و' links the two parallel predicates, and 'روشن‌روان' is a compound adjective 'bright‑soul/clear‑minded' parallel to 'بیدار دل'.
segments.s144-g7: This copular clause uses the pronoun 'تویی' ('you are') at the end, a normal classical Persian order. The speaker attributes two roles to the addressee: 'دل' (the heart) and the compound 'پشت گردان' — here 'پشت' (rear/back) plus 'گردان' (participial/adjectival sense 'turning/guarding'), forming an epithet meaning roughly 'the one who turns/guards the rear' or 'rear‑protector' of 'ایران' (Iran).
segments.s144-g8: 'به' is instrumental/associative here ('by/with' or 'as'), introducing two metaphoric possessions: 'چنگال' (claw/talon) and 'نیروی' (strength/force). 'شیران' (literally 'lions') is used metaphorically for brave warriors; the clause closes again with the copula 'تویی' ('you are').
segments.s144-g17: تویی is the full-form copular pronoun 'thou art' (you are). The predicate that follows — از همه بد به ایران پناه — is a verbless nominal phrase: از همه بد ('worse than all' / 'the worst of all') modifies پناه ('refuge/protection') and به ایران marks the beneficiary 'for Iran'. The order is emphatic (pronoun + comment) typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s144-g18: ز تو is a causal/agentive prepositional phrase 'because of you / from you' placed before the verb; برفرازند is a poetic 3rd-person plural present/optative form meaning 'they are held high' or 'they raise (themselves/it) aloft'; گردان کلاه ('the troop [of] hats') is an inverted noun phrase where گردان ('troop') and کلاه ('hats') form the scene (i.e. 'the troop's hats'). The verb precedes its noun-phrase object/agent here due to poetic inversion.
segments.s144-g19: گزاینده کاری بد is a nominal phrase 'the chooser (گزاینده) of a bad/deed (کاری بد)' functioning as subject. آمد is the simple past 'came' and به پیش is a prepositional phrase 'into the presence of / before (me)'. Note کاری here is a noun formed from کار; the gloss as a verbal sense list does not match its nominal use.
segments.s144-g20: کز here functions as 'from / because of' (causal). اندیشه آن = 'that one's thought' (اندیشه 'thought' + آن 'that/he'). دل م is the heart with the enclitic م (= my) split in the token list; گشت is the past of گشتن 'to become/turn' meaning 'became', and ریش is used predicatively ('beard/bristle'), a metaphorical or idiomatic result phrase ('my heart became bearded' i.e. changed in feeling). The clause shows older/poetic morphology and a predicative nominal after گشت.
segments.s144-g21: نشستند is the past plural verb 'they sat down / they sat' (note: the provided lemma senses listed washing-related meanings and do not match; treat as 'sat'). گردان is the subject 'troop(s)'; به پیشم 'in my presence / before me' is the locative phrase and به هم 'together' adds manner. Word order places the finite verb before subject and complements for poetic effect.
segments.s144-g22: چو is a temporal conjunction here 'when'. خواندیم is the 1st-person plural past 'we read/recited'. آن نامه گژدهم is the direct object 'that letter of Gozdaham' (آن = that; نامه = letter; گژدهم = proper name). The clause is a straightforward temporal subordinate ('when we read that letter').
segments.s144-g23: چنان باد is an optative formula 'may it be so' (باد here is the optative particle, not 'wind'). کاندر جهان 'in the world' gives the domain, and جز تو کس literally 'except you someone' (i.e. 'no one but you' when combined with the negative that follows). This is a typical poetic inversion: optative particle + locative phrase + exclusion.
segments.s144-g24: نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative of بودن 'to be' ('may there not be / there be none'); it negates the existence of anyone except the addressee. به هر کار 'in every/any affair' is a locative/applicative phrase, and فریادرس is the predicate noun 'a rescuer/succorer'. Together the clause says 'there be no rescuer in any matter (except you)'.
segments.s144-g41: ازو marks the source/owner (’from him’). نامه بستد uses the past form بستد (classical past) = ‘sealed/bound a letter’ (بستن = to bind/seal); here بستد is 3rd-person past. به کردار آب is a simile/adverbial phrase ‘in the manner of water’ (به + کردار = ‘in the manner/way of’), saying he put/sent it off as water would run.
segments.s144-g42: برفت is a poetic/classical past form meaning ‘he went off/left’ (here the ordinary verb رفت). و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. نجست is past of جستن used here in the sense ‘sought’ (transitive); ایچ negates the object (‘no/not any’), so نجست ایچ آرام و خواب = ‘sought no rest and sleep.’ Word order is compact and poetic: verb before subject is common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s144-g43: چو is a temporal conjunction ‘when’. نزدیکی is a noun ‘nearness/vicinity’ and combines with زابلستان to mean ‘the vicinity of Zabulistan’. رسید is the 3rd-person past ‘arrived/reached’; together the clause means ‘when (he) reached near Zabulistan.’
segments.s144-g44: خروش طلایه is a genitive construction ‘the cry/shout of the vanguard’ (noun + noun). به + دستان is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into the hands’ (here idiomatically ‘reached/was received’), and رسید again is past ‘arrived/reached.’ The sequence says that the vanguard’s cry reached/was received (by those on hand).
segments.s144-g45: تهمتن is the proper name (Tahmatan). پذیره here denotes ‘reception/welcoming’ used predicatively; شدش is the past form شد with the enclitic -ش = ‘him/it,’ so پذیره شدش = ‘he was received/welcomed (him).’ با سپاه is an instrumental/adverbial phrase ‘with an army,’ specifying how/with whom he was received. The construction is compact: noun + nominal predicate + past verb with enclitic object.
segments.s144-g46: نهادند is 3rd-person plural past of نهادن ‘they placed/put.’ بر سر بزرگان is the prepositional phrase ‘on the heads of the great ones’ (بر = on/upon, سر = head, بزرگان = great men). کلاه is the direct object ‘hats.’ The order places the verb before the object phrase (poetic flexibility) but the meaning is straightforward: ‘they put hats on the heads of the great.’
segments.s144-g47: پیاده is an adverb/adjective ‘on foot’ (or ‘infantry’); شدش is past شد with enclitic -ش = ‘him,’ giving a classical verbal phrase meaning ‘he became on foot’ i.e. ‘he dismounted.’ The following name گیو is the subject (word order inverted for poetry). و گردان بهم links ‘and the troop’ (گردان) with the adverb بهم ‘together,’ i.e. ‘and the troop gathered together.’
segments.s144-g48: هر آنکس که بودند is a relative clause ‘whichever persons they were’ (هر = every/whichever; آنکس که بودند = those who were). از بیش و کم is an idiomatic pair ‘of more and less,’ meaning regardless of rank or number (i.e. whether many or few). The clause qualifies who dismounted/gathered: every man, great or small.
segments.s144-g57: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that') referring back to a person; ماننده is a simile marker 'like' and directly modifies the following name سام. گرد is used here as an intransitive verb 'to go about/ride about' (a poetic usage), and از مهان means 'among/from the guests' with از marking the group in which he rides.
segments.s144-g58: سواری is the noun 'a rider/horseman' serving as the subject; پدید آمد is a two-word verbal expression meaning 'appeared' (پدید 'manifest' + آمد 'came'), and اندر جهان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the world' (classical اندر = 'in').
segments.s144-g59: از آزادگان is a prepositional phrase 'from/among the free (noblemen)'; این ('this') refers back to the foregoing statement; نباشد is the negative present copula 'is not' (classical form), and شگفت is a noun 'a marvel/wonder' used in the negated predicate ('is not a wonder').
segments.s144-g60: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from the Turks'; چنین modifies یاد as 'such' ('such a remembrance/renown'); یاد here means 'remembrance/renown' and نتوان گرفت is the negative potential construction 'cannot be taken/obtained' (نتوان = 'cannot', گرفت = 'take/obtain').
segments.s144-g61: من is the topic/pronoun 'I'; از + دخت شاه سمنگان is a genitive/source phrase 'from/by the daughter of the king of Samangan' (دخت 'daughter' governed by شاه 'king' of سمنگان); یکی follows to mean 'one' or 'a certain one' (here 'one [child]').
segments.s144-g62: پسر دارم is a straightforward present-tense possession 'I have a son' (دارم = 'I have'); و connects clauses 'and'; باشد او کودکی is a nominal clause 'he is a child' where باشد functions as the copula 'is' and the pronoun او follows it (classical order) with کودکی as the predicate noun 'a child'.
segments.s144-g63: هنوز 'yet/still' precedes the clause; آن گرامی is 'that noble/esteemed one' (anaphoric); نداند is the negative present 'does not know' (classical third person), and که جنگ introduces the content clause 'that (regarding) war/fighting' — i.e. 'does not yet know war/know how to fight.'
segments.s144-g64: توان کرد is a modal+verb cluster 'be able to do/fight' (توان = can/ability, کرد = do); باید is the impersonal 'it is necessary/one must'; گه here means 'occasion/when' (the time/occasion on which the necessity falls), and نام و ننگ 'name (reputation) and shame' state what is at stake — together: 'one must be able to fight when reputation and dishonor are concerned.'
segments.s144-g81: This clause expresses prohibition using an older word order. بدین تیزی (literally 'by/with this swiftness') is an adverbial phrase (بدین = 'with this', تیزی = 'swiftness/speed'). اندر نیاید به جنگ is a negative jussive construction: نیاید is an optative/jussive form meaning 'should not go/come', and اندر ... به جنگ = 'into / to the battle'. Together: 'With such swiftness one should not go into battle.'
segments.s144-g82: نباید is an impersonal modal/negative ('one must not' / 'it ought not to be'); گرفتن is the infinitive 'to take/undertake' used as the complement of نباید. چنین کار تنگ is a noun phrase ('such a narrow/tight affair/deed') serving as the object of گرفتن. The whole clause = 'one should not undertake such a narrow (i.e. risky/inappropriate) action.'
segments.s144-g83: به + می‌دست is an instrumental/locative phrasing ('with / in the hand'); می‌دست is an archaic/periphrastic expression meaning 'holding' or 'with (the) hand'. بردند is a past simple plural 'they took/carried', and مستان شدند is 'they became drunk' (مستان = drunk/inebriate; شدند = became). Grammatically this is two coordinated predicates joined by و.
segments.s144-g84: ز یاد is a prepositional phrase 'from (their) remembrance / having forgotten'. سپهبد is the noun 'spahbed' (the commander). به دستان شدند uses به + دستان ('to/towards tales/stories' or 'into storytelling') plus شدند 'they became', i.e. they fell to telling tales; the clause describes the consequence of their drunkenness. Here به marks motion/tendency toward the following noun phrase.
segments.s144-g85: دگر روز is a temporal phrase 'another/next day' (دگر = another); شبگیر is an epithet/noun 'night‑visitor' or 'one who came at night'. هم 'also' reinforces inclusion, and پرخمار is an adjective 'deeply intoxicated' modifying that person. Word order is typical Classical Persian: temporal phrase + epithet + modifiers.
segments.s144-g86: بیامد is past 'he/they came'. تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (Tahmatan, 'the mighty one'). برآراست is a past-form verbal adjective/verb meaning 'arranged/put in order' and takes کار ('the affair') as its object; here the verb phrase is transitive and describes that Tahmatan came and arranged the matter.
segments.s144-g87: ز مستی is a causal prepositional phrase 'from/because of drunkenness'. هم آن روز 'that day too' gives time and inclusiveness, and باز ایستاد is a compound past 'he stood back / refrained again' (باز = again; ایستاد = stood/stopped). The clause says that due to drunkenness he once more abstained or stayed put that day.
segments.s144-g88: دوم روز is a temporal phrase 'on the second day'. رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' (the notion of leaving), and نیامدش is a past negative impersonal/experiential form 'the thought did not come to him / it did not occur to him' (نیامد + direct object clitic -ش). یاد here is 'the thought/memory/idea'. Altogether: 'On the second day the idea of going did not come to him.'
segments.s144-g97: به + place phrase marks direction/place: here به … زابلستان‌گر = 'toward/at Zabulistan'. درنگ is a noun 'delay, tarrying', and آوریم is a 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive/jussive of آوردن used idiomatically to mean 'let us cause/let us make' or 'we will (do)'. Together the clause means 'if we tarry/stop at Zabulistan' (the verb is in the hortative/optative sense).
segments.s144-g98: ز here = 'because of/on account of'. می‌باز is a descriptor for a dissipated/wine‑loving person; پیگار is 'strife/battle' and جنگ is 'war'. آوریم repeats the 1st‑plurable verb 'آوردن' in the same hortative/causative sense 'we will cause/bring about'. The whole segment states the consequence: 'because of the wine‑fond man we will raise strife and bring about war.'
segments.s144-g99: شود is the present/impersonal form of شدن 'to become' used here to mean 'will become / will be'. شاه ایران is the subject 'the king of Iran' and به ما = 'toward us / against us'; خشمگین is the predicate adjective 'angry'. Word order is subject–predicate with the indirect object phrase after the subject.
segments.s144-g100: ز = 'because of / from'. ناپاک رایی = 'corrupt/impure counsel/opinion' (compound noun phrase with adjective before noun). درآید is a 3rd‑singular subjunctive/indicative of درآمدن 'to arise/come forth', and بکین is a poetic form meaning 'vengeance / taking vengeance' (here an outcome). Together: 'from corrupt counsel vengeance will arise' or 'he will be led to vengeance by corrupt counsel.'
segments.s144-g101: بدو = 'to him' (dative pronoun) so the phrase 'بدو گفت رستم' means 'Rostam said to him'. که introduces an indirect speech clause 'that'. مندیش ازین is 'don't think / do not imagine from this' — مندیش is an imperative/negative-stem meaning 'do not suppose' (poetic/archaic), and ازین = 'from this' (از + این).
segments.s144-g102: که = 'that' (subordinator). با ما = 'with us'. نشورد is an archaic negative-present form meaning 'will not come / will not happen' (equivalent to نشود/نشد) and takes کس 'anyone' as its subject; اندر زمین = 'in/within the earth' = 'on earth'. The clause thus says 'that no one on earth will come with us.'
segments.s144-g103: بفرمود is a polite/epic past verb 'he ordered/commanded'. تا introduces purpose/result here ('so that / that they should'). رخش را (steed + object marker) is the direct object and زین کنند uses کنند (3rd‑pl present/subjunctive of کردن) with زین 'saddle' as object: 'that they saddle Rakhsh.'
segments.s144-g104: دم اندر دم is a reduplicated adverbial phrase 'moment by moment' (دم = 'moment'). نای is the reed‑flute (or a horn), رویین here means 'iron/metal' (descriptive adjective), and کنند (3rd‑pl present subjunctive) means 'they make/do'—together 'they sound/handle the iron reed continually.'
segments.s144-g89: سه دیگر سحرگه: a temporal adverbial placed before the verb meaning “on the third morning.” In classical Persian 'سه دیگر' functions like an ordinal/adverbial (“the third”), and 'سحرگه' (= at dawn) specifies time. بیاورد می: 'بیاورد' is the past-tense third-person singular of آوردن (“he brought”), and the direct object می (here the noun “wine”) follows the verb in this word order (verb + object), which is common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s144-g90: نیامد ورا یاد کاووس کی: 'نیامد' is past negative (“did not come”). 'ورا' is the unstressed enclitic/object pronoun (= 'to him'), placed after the verb as is normal in older Persian ('نیامد ورا' = 'did not come to him'). 'یاد کاووس' is a noun + genitive ('the memory/remembrance of Kavus'), with یاد as the head and کاووس in genitive relation. 'کی' here is an interrogative/adverbial particle (classical use) functioning as an emphatic/temporal 'when/ever' (rendered here as 'ever/at all').
segments.s144-g91: به‌روز چهارم برآراست گیو: 'به‌روز چهارم' is a temporal prepositional phrase 'on the fourth day' (classical spacing/orthography). 'برآراست' is a past-form verb meaning 'he arrayed/adorned (himself)' or 'he made ready', and the proper name گیو (Giv) follows the verb here (verb before subject is a common poetic/orderly pattern).
segments.s144-g92: چنین گفت با گرد سالار نیو: 'چنین گفت' is a fixed classical phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', and گفت here functions as 'said' in the narrative). 'با' introduces the person spoken to ('with/to'), and the phrase 'گرد سالار' is a compound/title meaning roughly 'chief of the (troop/host)' (گرد is an older/poetic element referring to a troop/array), so 'با گرد سالار نیو' = 'to/with the troop‑chief Niu' (نیو is a proper name). Word order places the addressee after the verb phrase.
segments.s144-g93: که کاووس تندست و هشیار نیست: 'که' is the conjunction 'that/for' introducing an explanatory clause. کاووس is the subject. تندست (from تندس) is an older adjective/noun used adjectivally meaning 'impetuous/rash'; 'و' = 'and'; 'هشیار نیست' is the negated copula with هشیار ('alert/sane') + نیست ('is not'), so 'is not clear‑headed/sober.'
segments.s144-g94: هم این داستان بر دلش خوار نیست: هم here is an additive/emphatic 'also/indeed.' 'این داستان' is the topicalized subject 'this tale.' 'بر دلش' is a classical locative/benefactive construction ('upon/for his heart') with the possessive clitic ش ('his'). 'خوار نیست' is a negated predicate: خوار = 'despised/worthless' and نیست = 'is not', so 'is not despised/unwelcome to his heart.' Note the classical use of بر + دل to mark what is acceptable/unacceptable 'to his heart.'
segments.s144-g95: غمی بود ازین کار و دل پرشتاب: 'غمی بود' is an existential past construction ('there was grief') with the indefinite noun غمی and past 'بود' ('was'). 'ازین کار' = 'from/of this affair' (classical fused اَز + این), giving the cause. 'دل پرشتاب' is a noun phrase 'a heart full of haste/agitation' (پر- prefixed to شتاب = 'full of haste'), describing the state.
segments.s144-g96: شده دور ازو خورد و آرام و خواب: 'شده' is the perfect/passive participial form 'has become' (linked to the previous 'دل پرشتاب شده' = 'his heart became agitated'). 'دور ازو خورد' literally 'it fell/become distant from him': ازو = 'from him', دور = 'distant', and خورد here is the light verb 'to befall/fall upon' (classical use of خوردن meaning 'to befall' or 'to come upon'). The trailing 'و آرام و خواب' names the states that follow—'and calm and sleep'—understood as having come upon him (subject omitted, typical in Persian ellipsis).
segments.s144-g73: ازان پس = a temporal phrase 'after that/thereupon' (ازان = 'thereupon', پس = 'after'). گراییم is a 1st-person-plural present/optative of گرائیدن/گِراختن 'to turn/steer' (here 'we turn/we steer'); نزدیک شاه follows and functions as spatial complement 'near the king' (Adverbial phrase after the verb).
segments.s144-g74: به گردان ایران نماییم راه is inverted: نماییم (1st‑pl. of نمودن 'to show/make') governs راه ('way/path') which comes at the end; به گردان ایران = 'toward the troop of Iran' (به = towards/into, گردان = troop, ایران = Iran). Together it yields 'we make our way toward the Iranian troop.'
segments.s144-g75: مگر ... نیست here introduces a conditional/hypothetical idea: 'مگر' (literary 'unless/if') with بخت رخشنده 'the radiant/shining fortune' and بیدار نیست 'is awake' (نیست is the negative copula, but in classical usage the construction implies 'if/so long as the fortunate star is awake'). Grammatically this is a protasis (condition) setting up the following clause.
segments.s144-g76: وگرنه = 'otherwise' or 'if not', but here it links the condition to the result: چنین کار دشوار نیست = 'such a deed/work is not difficult' (چنین = 'such', کار = 'deed/affair', دشوار = 'difficult', نیست = negation 'is not'). Together: if the shining fortune is awake, this task will not be hard.
segments.s144-g77: چو = 'when'; دریا به موج اندرآید = 'the sea comes/turns into a wave' (اندرآید present/subjunctive/epic-form 'enters/comes in'); ز جای = 'from its place' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place'). This is a temporal clause describing a sudden stirring.
segments.s144-g78: ندارد دم = 'has no moment/breath' (ندارد = 'does not have', دم used idiomatically as 'a moment/instant'); آتش تیزپای is a poetic epithet 'fiery swift‑footed (one)' where آتش qualifies the swift‑footedness. The whole means 'the fiery swift one does not stop even for a moment.'
segments.s144-g79: درفش مرا = 'my banner' (مـ- here is the clitic for 'me/my' attached to درفش; را is the object marker). چون ببیند ز دور = 'when he sees [it] from afar' (چون = 'when', ببیند = 3rd‑singular present/subjunctive of دیدن 'sees', ز دور = 'from a distance'). Word order places the object phrase before the temporal subordinate clause.
segments.s144-g80: دلش ماتم آرد = literal 'his heart brings mourning' — a causative/utterance pattern meaning 'his heart is filled with sorrow.' آرد is 3rd‑singular present of آوردن 'to bring'. به هنگام سور = 'at the time of the feast' (به هنگام = 'at the time of', سور = 'feast'); the contrast (mourning at a feast) is idiomatic and poetic.
segments.s144-g65: فرستادمش is the 1st‑person past verb 'I sent' with the object clitic -ش ('him/her/it') attached. زر و گوهر بسی is a coordinated noun phrase 'gold and jewels' modified by بسی ('much/a great deal'). Word order places the verb+clitic first, then the direct object phrase.
segments.s144-g66: بر here functions as a preposition meaning 'to/toward' (direction/recipient). مادر او is the noun phrase 'his/her mother' (مادر head, او genitive clitic). به دست کسی is an instrumental phrase 'by the hand of someone' where به takes the 'by' sense and کسی means 'someone'. The whole segment gives the recipient/means of delivery.
segments.s144-g67: چنین پاسخ آمد که is a fixed introductory clause 'such an answer came that...'؛ چنین modifies پاسخ ('such/so'), پاسخ is the noun 'answer', and آمد is the past tense 'came'. که introduces the subordinate clause whose subject begins with آن ارجمند ('that esteemed one').
segments.s144-g68: بسی modifies the negative verb برنیاید: بسی برنیاید means 'it will not suffice' or 'it will not be enough/appropriate'. برنیاید is a negative present/subjunctive form meaning 'does not/will not be fitting/occur'. که introduces the complement clause 'that (he) گردد بلند' where گردد is a present/subjunctive 'become' and بلند is the predicate adjective 'lofty/exalted.'
segments.s144-g69: همی is an older/proto‑Persian adverb indicating continuous/habitual action ('keeps/continually'). می‌خورد is the present habitual/continuous 'eats/drinks/feeds'. با لب شیربوی is a prepositional phrase literally 'with milk‑scented lip(s)' used idiomatically for nursing at the breast; با here marks accompaniment or the place/means of feeding.
segments.s144-g70: شود is a 3rd‑person singular present form of شدن ('become') used here to indicate a forthcoming change. بی‌گمان 'undoubtedly' and زود 'soon' are adverbs modifying the predicate, and پرخاشجوی is the predicate adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive.' The subject is omitted and refers back to the previously mentioned person/child.
segments.s144-g71: بباشیم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/hortative 'let us be / let us remain' (a polite/imprecise imperative). یک روز gives duration 'one day' and دم 'a breath/moment' is another temporal expression. برزنیم is a 1st‑person plural form derived from برزن (the plain/threshing‑floor) and here functions as a verbalized phrase 'let us be/go on the plain' (poetic usage where a place‑noun is used with verbal morphology).
segments.s144-g72: یکی can mean 'one (person/of us)' or 'someone' here implying 'let one [of us]...'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon', لب is the head noun 'lip/brink', modified by خشک 'dry' and نم 'dew/moisture' (لب خشک نم = 'the dry brink/edge of the moisture'). The final برزنیم repeats the hortative 1st‑person plural verb form; in such poetic lines the exact agreement can be flexible and the phrase reads as an exhortation about positioning someone (one person) at that place while the group remains.

sentences.s144-s53: When the Zabul horsemen heard the ney, they rose from their places with rags and cuirasses.
sentences.s144-s17: Then he said to Giv, "Make a smoke signal;" the rider's reins must be set to advantage.
sentences.s144-s18: You must come near Rostam; do not remain in Zabul, nor fall asleep.
sentences.s144-s19: If night comes, return by day; tell him that the battle has become tight/critical.
sentences.s144-s20: Otherwise this man is exalted; one cannot count the ill‑minded as lowly.
sentences.s144-s13: In that manner they saw Niv's troop, and that the noble Giv was coming before you.
sentences.s144-s14: When you read the letter, by day and by night, do not tell the tale with your lips wide open.
sentences.s144-s15: Unless you, with very clever horsemen, drive them from Zabul, you will raise a cry.
sentences.s144-s16: Because Gozdahm remembered him in that way, there should be no comrade beside him except you.
sentences.s144-s25: From the horse dismounted the famed-speaker; he asked about Iran and about the king.
sentences.s144-s26: They moved from the road toward Rostam's iwan; they paused a moment and then struck.
sentences.s144-s27: He told what he had heard and handed over the letter; he spoke for a while about Sohrab's memory.
sentences.s144-s28: When Tahmatan heard and read the letter, he laughed and remained astonished at that affair.
sentences.s144-s5: Opener of the bonds of the plains, taker of the frontier of Mazandaran.
sentences.s144-s6: Because of your mace the sun becomes weeping; because of your sword Anahita is burned.
sentences.s144-s7: Like the dust trailing after your steed there is no Nile; there is no elephant in the world that equals you.
sentences.s144-s8: Your lasso bound a lion; your spear uprooted a mountain from its root.
sentences.s144-s1: He sent a letter; then the king wrote to the renowned Rostam.
sentences.s144-s2: First he praised the Creator — the sovereign of the world and nurtured by fate.
sentences.s144-s3: Then he praised the champion again, (saying) be wakeful of heart and clear‑minded.
sentences.s144-s4: You are the heart and the rear‑protector of Iran; you are (likewise) the claw and the strength of the brave.
sentences.s144-s9: You are, above all, the worst refuge for Iran; because of you the troop's caps are held aloft.
sentences.s144-s10: A chooser of evil deeds came before me; from that one's thought my heart became (as if) bearded.
sentences.s144-s11: The troops sat together before me when we read that letter of Gozdaham.
sentences.s144-s12: May it be such that in the world no one except you is a rescuer in any affair.
sentences.s144-s21: He sealed a letter from him, set it like water, and went off, seeking neither rest nor sleep.
sentences.s144-s22: When he reached the vicinity of Zabulistan, the shout of the vanguard reached the hands (i.e., was heard/received).
sentences.s144-s23: Tahmatan was received (welcomed) with the army; they placed hats upon the heads of the great men.
sentences.s144-s24: Giv dismounted on foot, and the troops gathered together — every one of them, whether more or fewer.
sentences.s144-s29: A horseman appeared in the world who, like Sam, rode about among the guests.
sentences.s144-s30: Among the free this is no wonder; one cannot obtain such renown from the Turks.
sentences.s144-s31: I have one (son) by the daughter of the king of Samangan, and he is a child.
sentences.s144-s32: That noble one does not yet know war — that one must be able to fight when name and shame are at stake.
sentences.s144-s41: With such speed one must not enter into battle; one should not undertake such a narrow/dangerous affair.
sentences.s144-s42: They took it in their hands and became intoxicated; forgetting the spahbed, they turned to tales (storytelling).
sentences.s144-s43: On another day the night‑visitor also came, heavily intoxicated; Tahmatan came and set the affair in order.
sentences.s144-s44: Because of intoxication he again that day refrained/held back; on the second day the thought of going did not come to him.
sentences.s144-s49: If we halt in Zabulistan, we will, because of the wine‑fond man, bring about strife and war.
sentences.s144-s50: The king of Iran will be angry with us, and from corrupt counsel vengeance will arise.
sentences.s144-s51: Rostam said to him, 'Do not think that anyone on earth will join us.'
sentences.s144-s52: He commanded that they saddle Rakhsh, and, moment by moment, sound the iron reed.
sentences.s144-s45: On the third dawn he brought wine; not even the memory of Kavus came to him.
sentences.s144-s46: On the fourth day Giv arrayed himself; thus he spoke with the troop‑chief Niu.
sentences.s144-s47: For Kavus is impetuous and not clear‑headed; besides, this tale is not despised by his heart.
sentences.s144-s48: There was sorrow from this affair, and his heart became hurried; it departed from him and (so) calm and sleep fell upon him.
sentences.s144-s37: After that we turned toward the king and made our way to the troop of Iran.
sentences.s144-s38: Provided the shining fortune is awake, such a deed will not be difficult.
sentences.s144-s39: When the sea breaks into waves, the fiery swift‑footed one does not pause even for a moment.
sentences.s144-s40: When he sees my banner from afar, his heart is filled with mourning even at a feast.
sentences.s144-s33: I sent him/her a great deal of gold and jewels, delivered into his/her mother's hands by someone's hand.
sentences.s144-s34: Such an answer came: that it would not be enough for that noble one to become exalted.
sentences.s144-s35: He keeps feeding at milk‑scented lips; no doubt he will soon become quarrelsome.
sentences.s144-s36: Let us stay one day and linger a while on the plain; let one (of us) go to the dry brink of the dew on the plain.
